text: Cycling to work just 3 days a week can save over 1,500 kg of CO₂ per year.
A single round-trip flight from New York to London produces about 1.6 tonnes of CO₂ per passenger.
Public transit produces 45% less CO₂ per mile than driving alone.
If you drive, keeping your tires properly inflated can improve fuel efficiency by 3%.
Walking or cycling for trips under 3 km could eliminate 5% of all transport emissions.
Beef production generates 60 kg of CO₂ per kg of meat — 20x more than plant proteins.
If food waste were a country, it would be the third-largest emitter of greenhouse gases.
Going vegan for one year saves approximately 1.5 tonnes of CO₂ equivalent.
Buying local, seasonal produce can reduce your food's carbon footprint by up to 30%.
One-third of all food produced globally is wasted — that's 1.3 billion tonnes per year.
LED bulbs use 75% less energy than incandescent bulbs and last 25 times longer.
Standby power (phantom load) accounts for 5-10% of residential electricity use.
Lowering your thermostat by just 1°C can reduce heating costs by 10%.
Solar panels can offset 3-4 tonnes of CO₂ per year for an average home.
Washing clothes in cold water can save up to 500 kg of CO₂ per year.
The fashion industry produces 10% of global carbon emissions — more than aviation and shipping combined.
Buying one second-hand item instead of new saves an average of 5.7 kg of CO₂.
A new smartphone generates about 70 kg of CO₂ during manufacturing.
Choosing products with minimal packaging can reduce your waste by 40%.
Repairing items instead of replacing them can save both money and up to 50 kg of CO₂ per item.
The average person's carbon footprint is about 4-8 tonnes of CO₂ per year globally.
Trees absorb about 22 kg of CO₂ per year — plant one to offset your daily emissions.
The Paris Agreement aims to limit global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.
Small daily changes can reduce your carbon footprint by 20-30% within a year.
Composting food scraps can divert 30% of household waste from landfills.
